perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information to its Representatives (who may then disclose that Co nfidential Information to other of its Representatives) only if those Representatives are required to have the Confidential Information for the purpose s in this Agreement. Before doing so, each Party must ensure that affiliates and Representatives are required to protect the Confidential Information on terms consistent with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information to its Representatives (who may then disclose that Co nfidential Information to other of its Representatives). Before doing so, each Party must ensure that affiliates and Representatives are required to protect the Confidential Information on terms consistent with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing "only if those Representatives are required to have the Confidential Information for the purpose s in this Agreement" from section 3.c eliminates the condition that Representatives can only have access if necessary for the agreement's purpose. This creates a contradiction because section 3.a states "the Parties will use and disclose the other’s Confidential Information only for purposes of evaluating and engaging in an actual or potential business relationship with each other.". With this pertubation the Representatives could now potentially access Confidential Information for other purposes which is directly contrdicting what is stated in the begining of section 3.a.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In general. Subject to the other terms of this Agreement, each of us agrees: Except as required under any court order or law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act and Ralph M. Brown Act (California Government Code section 54950 et seq .), subpoena, or any other legally permitted or required disclosure, the Parties agree not to disclose the other’s Confidential Information to third parties except to those employees of a recipient who are required to have the information for the purposes in this Agreement, Representatives (defined below), and except as otherwise allowed in this Agreement ; and The Parties will use and disclose the other’s Confidential Information only for purposes of evaluating and engaging in an actual or potential business relationship with each other.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that representatives can only have access if necessary for the agreement's purpose, but the changed text removes this condition. The contradicted text in section 3.a explicitly states that "The Parties will use and disclose the other’s Confidential Information only for purposes of evaluating and engaging in an actual or potential business relationship with each other.". By removing the condition on Representatives' access, the modified text now allows them to potentially access Confidential Information for other purposes, which directly contradicts the restriction in section 3.a, creating a strong in-text contradiction based on the 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that they cannot control the incoming information the other Party will disclose in the course of the business relationship between the Parties , or what each Party’s Representatives will remember, even without notes or other aids. Each Party agrees that use of information in its Representatives’ unaided memories in the development or deployment of the Parties’ respective products or services does not create liability for that Party under this Agreement or trade secret law.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that use of information in its Representatives’ unaided memories in the development or deployment of the Parties’ respective products or services does not create liability for that Party under this Agreement or trade secret law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "Each Party acknowledges that they cannot control the incoming information the other Party will disclose in the course of the business relationship between the Parties , or what each Party’s Representatives will remember, even without notes or other aids." from section 3.c now implies that each party is fully responsible for controlling the information disclosed and what representatives remember, thus introducing a contradiction. The original text provided an explicit understanding that controlling all incoming information and memories isn't fully possible, limiting liability. Now, with the absence of this phrase, a breach argument could be made, contradicting the intention of limiting liability originally established in the section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Sharing Confidential Information with affiliates and Representatives. For the purposes of this Agreement, a “Representative” is an employee, contractor, vendor, service provider, advisor or consultant of a Party or a Party’s respective affiliates including, but not limited to, Science Applications International Corporation ( “SAIC”) and Atos Governmental IT Outsourcing Services, LLC (“Atos”) and each of their respective employees , affiliates , and subcontractors . Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information to its Representatives (who may then disclose that Co nfidential Information to other of its Representatives) only if those Representatives are required to have the Confidential Information for the purpose s in this Agreement. Before doing so, each Party must ensure that affiliates and Representatives are required to protect the Confidential Information on terms consistent with this Agreement. Neither Party is required to restrict work assignments of Representatives who have had access to Confidential Information. Each Party agrees that use of information in its Representatives’ unaided memories in the development or deployment of the Parties’ respective products or services does not create liability for that Party under this Agreement or trade secret law. Both Parties mutually agree to limit what each Party discloses to the other accordingly.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that parties cannot control all incoming information or what representatives remember, thus limiting liability related to that information. The changed text removes this explicit acknowledgement, implying that each party is responsible for controlling information and memories. This creates a contradiction because the 'Contradicted Text' section discusses sharing confidential information with Representatives and limiting liability for the use of Representatives' unaided memories. By removing the original acknowledgement, the changed text creates an environment where a breach could be argued for something that was originally intended to be protected. Thus it goes against the spirit of the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq . If a Party is required to disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information pursuant to applicable law, statute, or regulation , or court order, that Party will give to the other written notice of the order or request mandating disclosure of the Confidential Information in order to provide a reasonable opportunity for the other Party to object to such disclosure of its Confidentia l Information and seek a protective order or appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq .
perturbation.explanation: By removing the second half of the paragraph in Section 3.d, it omits the crucial step of providing written notice and opportunity to object if a party is legally required to disclose confidential information. This directly conflicts with and undermines the purpose of the following paragraph, which describes the process the County follows when receiving a California Public Records Act request, which includes notification to allow Microsoft to seek protection from disclosure. The modified text implies immediate disclosure is permissible, while the following paragraph outlines a procedure that expects a notification process before the potential disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: By entering into this Agreement, Microsoft expressly acknowledges that the County is a government entity subject to the California Public Records Act. If the County receives a request pursuant to the California Public Records Act for Confidential Information that Microsoft has designated as “confidential ” in writing , the County will notify Microsoft of the request, so that Microsoft can seek protection from disclosure by a court of comp etent jurisdiction. Notwithstanding any other provisions of this Agreement, the County may in its discretion disclose Microsoft’s Confidential Information in the County’s possession without breach of this Agreement if the County notifies Microsoft of the pending California Public Records Act request and Microsoft fails to seek and obtain protection from disclosure by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 3.d, by removing the requirement to provide written notice and opportunity to object before disclosing confidential information, directly contradicts the paragraph addressing California Public Records Act requests. The contradicted text explicitly states that the County will notify Microsoft of such requests, allowing Microsoft to seek protection from disclosure. The modified text implies immediate disclosure is permissible, while the contradicted paragraph outlines a notification process. This creates a strong in-text contradiction and uncertainty regarding the disclosure procedure.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding any other provisions of this Agreement, the County may in its discretion disclose Microsoft’s Confidential Information in the County’s possession without breach of this Agreement if the County notifies Microsoft of the pending California Public Records Act request and Microsoft fails to seek and obtain protection from disclosure by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding any other provisions of this Agreement, the County may in its discretion disclose Microsoft’s Confidential Information in the County’s possession without breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the condition "if the County notifies Microsoft of the pending California Public Records Act request and Microsoft fails to seek and obtain protection from disclosure by a court of competent jurisdiction" from Section 3.d, this change creates a direct contradiction. Before, the County could disclose without breach *only if* they notified Microsoft and Microsoft failed to act. Now, they can disclose in their discretion without any notification requirement. This conflicts with the intent established earlier that the County should provide Microsoft a chance to object and protect its information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information if required to comply with a court order, law, or other government demand that has the force of law including, but not limited to, the California Public Records Act , California Government Code section 6250 et seq . By entering into this Agreement, Microsoft expressly acknowledges that the County is a government entity subject to the California Public Records Act. If the County receives a request pursuant to the California Public Records Act for Confidential Information that Microsoft has designated as “confidential ” in writing , the County will notify Microsoft of the request, so that Microsoft can seek protection from disclosure by a court of comp etent jurisdiction. Notwithstanding any other provisions of this Agreement, the County may in its discretion disclose Microsoft’s Confidential Information in the County’s possession without breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a condition from the original text regarding when the County can disclose Microsoft's confidential information without breach of agreement. Originally, the County could only disclose if they notified Microsoft of a pending California Public Records Act request and Microsoft failed to seek protection. The changed text allows the County to disclose at its discretion without any notification requirement, creating a direct contradiction with the contradicted text, which states the County 'will notify Microsoft of the request, so that Microsoft can seek protection from disclosure.' Now, according to the changed text, the County does not need to notify Microsoft, creating a direct contradiction, that makes both terms impossible to enforce simultaneously.

perturbation.original_text: Except as permitted above and unless otherwise required by applicable law or court order , neither Party will use or disclose the other’s Confidential Information for five years after it is received . The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period. This duty to withhold Confidential Information survives any expiration or termination of this Agreement as provided herein .
perturbation.changed_text: Except as permitted above and unless otherwise required by applicable law or court order , neither Party will use or disclose the other’s Confidential Information for five years after it is received . This duty to withhold Confidential Information survives any expiration or termination of this Agreement as provided herein .
perturbation.explanation: Removing the sentence "The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period" from section 4 creates a contradiction because the remaining text states a specific time period for keeping confidentiality, but then lacks the crucial exception. Without it, the agreement might be interpreted as overriding any legal obligation to protect confidential information for a longer duration (e.g., if trade secret law requires indefinite protection). This in-text contradiction makes it unclear whether the five-year period is absolute or subject to legal overrides, thus increasing the probability of a disagreement with legal precedence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Length of Confidential Information obligations. Except as permitted above and unless otherwise required by applicable law or court order , neither Party will use or disclose the other’s Confidential Information for five years after it is received . The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period. This duty to withhold Confidential Information survives any expiration or termination of this Agreement as provided herein .
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the sentence "The five-year time period does not apply if applicable law requires a longer period." This creates a direct contradiction with the original intention, expressed in the contradicted text, that the five-year period is *not* absolute, but subject to legal requirements. Without this sentence, the contract implies a fixed five-year confidentiality period, even if law mandates longer protection, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction. This aligns with the Omissions – In Text Contradiction definition.
